title: Needle-felted flower corsage
subtitle: A three-petal felt flower with a wool-wrapped stem, suitable as a brooch or hair pin
excerpt: Needle-felt three individual petals from thin discs of merino roving, shape them while slightly damp, then join them with a few needle stabs at the base. A small wrapped wire stem and a pin back complete a wearable flower corsage for a coat lapel or hair clip.
sourceNotes: Needle-felted flower corsage forms are a well-established domestic craft. The petal construction method is observation-led.

content.text: Three individually needled petals and a simple joining technique produce a flower corsage that holds its shape without stiffener or wire. The petals are made from thin discs of roving needled flat on the foam mat, then shaped by hand while slightly damp from a brief rinse. The method takes about 90 minutes including drying time.

attrs.body: Always stab straight in and out at 90 degrees. The needle must enter foam, not a hard surface, on every stroke.
attrs.title: Needle safety

items.name: Brooch pin or hair clip for the back
Strong craft glue (or a few hand stitches with thread)

content.text: For each petal: pull off a piece of roving about 20 cm long. Fold it into a flat oval shape, roughly 6 x 4 cm, and place it on the foam mat. Using the medium

, needle the surface in a grid pattern, then turn the petal over and needle the reverse side. Continue until the petal holds its shape when you lift it and the fibres are compact enough to resist tearing. The fine needle smooths the surface once the basic structure is stable. Make three petals the same size.

Dampen each petal briefly under a running tap. While damp, fold the base of each petal slightly upward to give it a cupped, three-dimensional form. Pin to a foam block in the shaped position and leave to dry for 30 minutes. The wet felt sets to shape as it dries.

Arrange the three dry petals in a fan or trefoil arrangement, overlapping the bases. Needle through all three layers at the overlapping base point with the medium needle, driving it through the stack to lock the petals together. Work a small patch (about 1 cm across) at the base with 20-30 stabs to create a firm joint.
Step 4: Add the centre detail and pin back

Roll a small pinch of contrasting-colour roving into a tight ball the size of a large pea. Needle it into the centre of the joined petals with the fine needle, working around the perimeter of the ball to secure it without flattening it completely. Fix the brooch pin or hair clip to the back of the flower with a few drops of strong craft glue, or sew it on with a few hand stitches through the joint layer.

attrs.intro: Two problems specific to this pattern.

items.fix: The felt needs to be compacted before shaping will hold. If the petals spring back to flat after the first shaping attempt, needle them for another 5-10 minutes before re-dampening and shaping.
items.cause: Not enough drying time in the pinned position, or the felt was not sufficiently compressed before shaping.
items.symptom: Petals lose their cup shape after drying

items.fix: Drive the needle through the full thickness of the stacked petals at the joint point. Use 30-40 full-length stabs in a tight cluster at the base to create a firm mechanical lock.
items.cause: Not enough needle stabs through the joint. A few surface stabs do not bind the layers.
items.symptom: Petal joints are weak and the petals separate

attrs.heading: Common problems
